f219ec983fe5.short_history: Using soldiers to police Americans at home has been tightly limited since the Posse Comitatus Act of 1878 (18 U.S.C. 1385). Congress passed it after the Army was used to police the South and guard polling places during Reconstruction. The National Guard works in three modes. State active duty and Title 32 keep the Guard under the governor, and the Posse Comitatus limits do not bite there. Title 10 federalizes the Guard under the President, and the limits do apply. Washington, D.C. is its own case: under Article I, the D.C. Guard answers straight to the President, not a governor. The current run of domestic missions started in 2025, when the administration federalized California's Guard, sent troops into Los Angeles, and stood up the D.C. task force named here.
f219ec983fe5.constitutional_analysis: Start with why governments exist at all. The founders built one to secure 'domestic Tranquility' (Preamble) — law and order so ordinary people can live and work in peace. That is the first job, not an afterthought. The Constitution backs it with force. Article I, Section 8, Clause 15 lets Congress call up the militia 'to execute the Laws of the Union, suppress Insurrections and repel Invasions.' The founders used that power fast. They had already passed the Militia Acts of 1792. In 1794, armed men in western Pennsylvania shut down tax collection in the Whiskey Rebellion. A Supreme Court Justice, James Wilson, certified that the courts were overpowered. Then George Washington did something no President has done since: he put on his uniform and personally led nearly 13,000 militia into the field to restore order. That is the founding precedent — federal force, led by the President himself, upholding the law. Congress also wrote the standing exception by hand: the Insurrection Act, descended from the Calling Forth Act of 1792 and the Insurrection Act of 1807, lets the President deploy regular force when the law breaks down. Over D.C. the case is even plainer. Congress has 'exclusive Legislation in all Cases whatsoever' over the capital (Art. I, Sec. 8, Cl. 17), and the D.C. Guard is the President's to command. He is also Commander in Chief (Art. II, Sec. 2). When the Guard trains, supports police, or works under a governor's consent in Title 32, it is doing ordinary militia work the founders expected. The real founding limit comes from the same generation, and it is about standing armies, not modern courts. The founders feared troops used as everyday police. The Declaration of Independence charged the King with 'render[ing] the Military independent of and superior to the Civil Power' and with 'quartering large bodies of armed troops among us.' Hamilton warned in Federalist No. 8 that armies in the streets make a people 'familiar with the instruments of war.' Posse Comitatus is that fear written into law. So the founding yardstick is consistent. Federal force to put down a real rebellion or execute the laws is squarely American — Washington proved it. Soldiers standing in as routine police, with no insurrection to suppress, is the thing the founders guarded against. The line is whether troops are restoring order against a genuine breakdown of law, or doing day-to-day policing without one.
f219ec983fe5.prediction: Expect these missions to continue while they stay in the lowest-friction modes: Title 32 with a governor's consent, or the D.C.-federal status used here. Watch these markers. Any new Insurrection Act proclamation would widen the scope and draw immediate court fights. Defense Department duty-status and funding orders show how long and how big. Whether missions spread to more cities shows the trend. Falsifiable read: a court order that halts a deployment and survives appeal points to pullback in that area; an Insurrection Act invocation points to escalation. Absent either, the most likely path is status-quo continuation through 2026 with occasional city-by-city expansion.

d112acf5a8ca.short_history: Temporary Protected Status comes from the Immigration Act of 1990 (8 U.S.C. 1254a). It lets the Secretary of Homeland Security shield people from a country that has become unsafe — war or disaster — from being sent home, and lets them work while the danger lasts. By design it is temporary and discretionary. The Secretary sets the term and can end it. In 2025 the administration moved to end TPS for roughly 300,000 Venezuelans and for Haitians. Recipients sued. The Supreme Court let the Venezuela termination proceed in October 2025, and the case over Haiti's status ran on to the ruling reported here.
d112acf5a8ca.constitutional_analysis: Every nation decides who may enter and who may stay. The founders took that as basic to being a sovereign country. The Constitution hands the call to the elected branches, not the courts. Congress sets the rule of naturalization (Art. I, Sec. 8, Cl. 4), and the President must 'take Care that the Laws be faithfully executed' (Art. II, Sec. 3). They acted on it early. The very first Congress passed the Naturalization Act of 1790. In 1798, Congress and President Adams went further with the Alien Friends Act and the Alien Enemies Act, claiming power to remove aliens outright — and the Alien Enemies Act is still on the books today. Madison explained the logic in Federalist No. 42: handling relations with foreigners belongs to the national government. On that record, control over who stays is a political-branch power, and Congress chose to hand the on-off switch for TPS to the Secretary by statute — even limiting how much courts may second-guess it. The founding-based limit is not that a judge dislikes the policy. It is the rule of law itself. Even an alien is a 'person,' and the executive may not suspend or dispense with a law Congress wrote. That principle is old and hard-won. The English Bill of Rights of 1689 stripped the Crown of the power to suspend laws, and the founders deliberately denied it to the President — the Take Care Clause is its mirror image. Madison in Federalist Nos. 47-48 warned against any branch swallowing another's job. So the founding question is narrow and concrete: is the administration following the steps Congress set in Section 1254a, or rewriting the statute on its own? If it follows the law, ending a temporary program is exactly what the law allows. If it skips the steps Congress required, that is the problem — not the policy.
d112acf5a8ca.prediction: With the Supreme Court resolving the core authority question in the government's favor, the remaining fight narrows to procedure — whether the termination followed the Administrative Procedure Act and met the statute's notice timelines. Watch these markers. DHS termination notices in the Federal Register and their effective dates. The wind-down period granted to Haitian beneficiaries. Any procedural challenge on remand. Whether Congress moves to lock in or block the change. Falsifiable read: if terminations take effect on the noticed dates with no fresh injunction, the policy is settled for this group once the wind-down closes. A win on the procedural claims would delay it but would not undo the underlying authority.

c4206117db7c.short_history: Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 (19 U.S.C. 1862) lets the President raise tariffs when the Commerce Department finds that imports threaten national security. President Trump first used it in March 2018, putting 10% on aluminum and 25% on steel. In February 2025 new proclamations reimposed and widened the duties, and copper was added in July 2025. The June 2026 proclamation reported here is the next step in that chain.
c4206117db7c.constitutional_analysis: Protecting American industry with tariffs is not a new idea — it is one of the oldest in the country. The second law the First Congress ever passed was the Tariff Act of 1789, which Washington signed on July 4, 1789. Its open purpose was to fund the government and to protect the young nation's manufacturers. Two years later, Treasury Secretary Alexander Hamilton wrote his Report on Manufactures (1791), arguing for protective tariffs to break Britain's grip on American industry and build jobs at home. The founding ideal is economic independence: a free country should make its own goods and not lean on foreign powers. So using tariffs to defend American workers and industry sits deep in the founding tradition. But the founders were just as clear about who pulls the lever. The power to lay duties is Congress's, in plain text: 'The Congress shall have Power To lay and collect Taxes, Duties, Imposts and Excises' (Art. I, Sec. 8, Cl. 1), and to regulate trade with foreign nations (Cl. 3). They treated the tariff and the purse as the people's business, handled by the branch closest to the people. Madison called the power over the purse the people's 'most complete and effectual weapon' (Federalist No. 58). Section 232 is Congress lending that lever to the President for 'national security.' Courts have allowed the loan — the 1928 J.W. Hampton case said Congress may delegate power as long as it gives an 'intelligible principle.' The founding-grounded question is how far the loan stretches. When one official sets sector-wide duties indefinitely, year after year, does it still serve the narrow national-security purpose Congress named — or has it quietly become free-standing trade policy that only Congress was supposed to set? That is the live tension, and it comes straight from the founders' decision to keep the tariff power in Congress's hands.
c4206117db7c.prediction: Section 232 tariffs have proven sticky. They have outlived more than one administration and survived attempts to roll them back. Watch these markers. Court dockets challenging the stacked tariffs. Any Supreme Court move to take up a tariff case on delegation grounds. Retaliation from the EU, Canada, and China. The volume of exclusion requests at Commerce. Falsifiable read: absent a losing ruling at the Supreme Court, expect the regime to hold and ratchet up rather than retreat. The main reversal risk is a court, not Congress.

f53992639dab.short_history: Congress created the Federal Reserve in 1913 and, in 1977, gave it a 'dual mandate': maximum employment and stable prices. The Fed's rate-setting committee, the FOMC, moves the federal funds rate. After inflation spiked in 2022 the Fed raised rates hard, and the 2025-2026 stretch has been a careful walk back down. That makes each FOMC statement and its quarterly rate projections — the 'dot plot' — a market-moving event. This release covers the June 16-17, 2026 meeting.
f53992639dab.constitutional_analysis: A national money institution was one of the first big constitutional fights in America. In 1790, Hamilton asked Congress to charter a Bank of the United States. Jefferson and Madison fought it, arguing the Constitution nowhere lists a power to create a bank, so Congress had no business doing it. Hamilton answered that the power to coin money and regulate its value (Art. I, Sec. 8, Cl. 5), joined to the Necessary and Proper Clause, carried the authority with it. Washington sided with Hamilton and signed the bank into law in 1791. The Supreme Court settled the point in McCulloch v. Maryland (1819): Congress may charter a national bank. So a central money institution is constitutional — but it was contested from the first day, and the founders cared above all about sound money. They had watched the Continental dollar inflate to nothing, and they wrote the coinage power to keep money honest. The live founding tension today is about control, not existence. The Constitution vests 'the executive Power' in one President (Art. II, Sec. 1). In the Decision of 1789, the First Congress — led by Madison — concluded that the President must be able to remove the officers who run the executive branch, so the buck stops with one accountable person. A Federal Reserve whose governors are shielded from removal sits in tension with that design. Courts have allowed independent agencies to exist, and they have treated the Fed as a special, historically rooted case. But measured against the founders' structure, the hard question stands: is steering the nation's money a core executive job that must answer to the elected President, or a special function Congress may wall off? The founders argued about the bank itself; we now argue about who controls it.
f53992639dab.prediction: Read the rate path from the Fed's own released projections, not from outside opinion. Watch these markers. The dot-plot median for end-2026. Whether the statement calls inflation 'elevated' or 'easing,' and the job market 'solid' or 'softening.' Incoming inflation (CPI/PCE) and jobs data. Market-implied odds of the next move (CME FedWatch). Falsifiable read: if the dots show more cuts and inflation keeps cooling, expect more easing ahead; a hot inflation print would freeze the path. Separately, watch any move to test whether a Fed governor can be removed — that institutional fight, more than the next quarter-point, is the real wildcard.

f77d2fc466ef.short_history: The professional federal civil service dates to the Pendleton Act of 1883, passed after a rejected job-seeker shot President Garfield. It replaced the old 'spoils system,' where each new President handed out government jobs to supporters. 'Schedule F' was created by executive order in October 2020. It would move policy-influencing career jobs into an at-will status the President can hire and fire at will. President Biden canceled it in January 2021. The second Trump administration brought it back on day one in 2025, and the 2026 final rule reported here, 'Schedule Policy/Career,' puts it into effect.
f77d2fc466ef.constitutional_analysis: The founders did not build a protected, hard-to-fire civil service. Under their design, the people who carry out executive jobs answered to the President, plain and simple. The reason was accountability. The Constitution vests 'the executive Power' in one President (Art. II, Sec. 1) and tells him to see that the laws are 'faithfully executed' (Art. II, Sec. 3). In 1789, the very first Congress faced the question head-on. Led by James Madison, it concluded that the President alone must be able to remove the officers under him — the 'Decision of 1789' — precisely so one accountable person owns how the executive branch behaves. Hamilton made the case in Federalist No. 70: good government needs 'energy' and 'unity' in the executive, one responsible head the people can hold to account. The Supreme Court built on that founding decision in Myers v. United States (1926). By that measure, a President reasserting control over policy-level employees is working with the grain of the founding design, not against it. The tenured civil service is the newer thing — it arrived in 1883, almost a century after the founding. There is a real founding-based counter-argument, and it also comes from the founders, not from modern judges. Congress, not the President, is given the job of structuring the offices of government (the Necessary and Proper Clause, and the Appointments Clause, Art. II, Sec. 2, Cl. 2, which lets Congress set the terms of inferior officers). And the founders despised the spoils system in advance: Hamilton warned in Federalist No. 76 against appointments driven by 'personal attachment' and favoritism. So the honest founding question is which danger is worse — an executive branch that will not answer to the elected President, or a return to hiring and firing by loyalty. Both worries trace straight back to the founders.
f77d2fc466ef.prediction: Expect immediate court challenges, as in the last cycle. Watch these markers. Suits by federal-employee unions and any order pausing the rule. How many positions agencies actually reclassify. Whether Congress moves to protect the civil service by statute or to defund the change through spending bills. Falsifiable read: if courts let it proceed and agencies start reclassifying, expect a measurable shift of policy-tier jobs to at-will status within six to twelve months. A pause or a controlling appellate ruling would stall it. A lasting answer probably waits on the Supreme Court.

0c0eef3320b7.short_history: Federal vaccine guidance comes through HHS and the CDC's advisory committee (ACIP), which has recommended a childhood schedule since 1964. But the federal government does not make anyone get a shot — the states do, through school-entry rules. A presidential memorandum in December 2025 ordered the 'core' childhood schedule realigned to match other developed countries. The executive order reported here carries that out through HHS.
0c0eef3320b7.constitutional_analysis: Under the founders' design, health and safety are mainly a state job, not a federal one. The Tenth Amendment is blunt: powers 'not delegated to the United States... are reserved to the States.' Madison spelled out what that means in Federalist No. 45 — the federal government's powers are 'few and defined,' while the states keep authority over 'the lives, liberties, and properties of the people.' Public health sits squarely in that reserved space, and the founding generation lived it. When yellow fever ravaged Philadelphia in 1793 — the worst epidemic of the era, in the nation's own capital — the federal government did not run the response. Officials, including Washington, simply left town, and the city and Pennsylvania handled the quarantines and relief. More than a century later the Supreme Court confirmed the rule in Jacobson v. Massachusetts (1905): the power to require vaccination belongs to the states. Measured against that tradition, this action stays inside the lines as long as it is only a recommendation. The President may set the advice of a federal agency he supervises (Art. II), and a recommendation forces nothing on any citizen or any state. The states stay free to keep their own school rules — and some will. The founding line gets crossed only if Washington uses money to force the states' hands. If federal funds — Medicaid, the Vaccines for Children program — are made conditional on adopting the new schedule, that turns a suggestion into pressure. The founders' federal-state balance does not let the national government do by funding what it cannot do by command.
0c0eef3320b7.prediction: Whether this matters depends on one thing: does it stay a recommendation, or does money get attached? Watch these markers. Publication of the revised schedule and any change in who sits on the advisory committee. Whether HHS attaches conditions to federal vaccine funding. How state health departments respond — expect a patchwork, with some states keeping the old schedule. Reactions from major medical groups like the American Academy of Pediatrics. Falsifiable read: if it stays advisory, expect a state-by-state split and little immediate change in what is required. If funding conditions are attached, expect federalism and spending-power lawsuits within months.

8143b1edc7bc.short_history: U.S.-Iran nuclear diplomacy ran through the 2015 deal known as the JCPOA, which was never a Senate-ratified treaty — it was an executive arrangement. President Trump pulled the U.S. out in 2018 and piled on 'maximum pressure' sanctions. After years of tension, including friction around the Strait of Hormuz, the June 2026 Memorandum of Understanding reported here marks a return to a negotiated framework. Like the last one, it is an executive instrument — an MOU — not a treaty the Senate has ratified.
8143b1edc7bc.constitutional_analysis: The founders gave one man the lead in talking to foreign nations, but never the power to bind the country alone. The President negotiates; the Senate must consent. The Treaty Clause requires 'the Advice and Consent of the Senate... two thirds of the Senators present' for a treaty (Art. II, Sec. 2, Cl. 2). That supermajority was a deliberate, high wall. Hamilton explained why in Federalist No. 75: joining the President and the Senate keeps any one person from entangling the whole nation in foreign affairs. Jay defended the same check in Federalist No. 64. They proved the system early with the Jay Treaty. Washington's envoy negotiated it in 1794; the Senate ratified it in 1795 by exactly the two-thirds needed, 20 votes to 10; and when the House tried to grab a role and demand the negotiating papers, Washington refused — establishing that the House does not get a treaty veto. That is the founding template: the President negotiates, the Senate consents, and a real commitment runs through that gate. So where does an MOU fall? If it binds nothing in U.S. law and simply rests on the President's recognized power to conduct diplomacy and manage sanctions, it stays in executive territory — and needs no Senate vote, because it commits nothing a later President cannot undo. The JCPOA's own on-again, off-again history shows exactly how reversible these are. The founding-based worry is the workaround: if major, lasting national commitments get routed through MOUs and executive deals precisely to skip the Senate's two-thirds check, that dodges the wall the founders built on purpose. The test is simple — does the instrument actually bind the nation (treaty territory, Senate required), or is it a reversible handshake (executive territory)?
8143b1edc7bc.prediction: An MOU is weak by design — it binds no future President and no Congress. Watch these markers. IAEA inspection access and verification reports. Iran's enrichment levels and centrifuge counts. Whether promised sanctions relief actually happens (OFAC delistings) versus stays on paper. Strait of Hormuz shipping traffic and marine-insurance rates as the real-world stress gauge. Whether the administration seeks formal congressional review or a binding instrument. Falsifiable read: if verification and reciprocal sanctions relief move on schedule, the framework holds in the near term. A verification dispute, a hidden-enrichment finding, or a Hormuz incident would break it fast. Only Senate ratification of a binding treaty would signal real durability — and that is unlikely.
